coredata.dc:title: Unleashing the potential of carbon dots bound Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X as an oxone activator for the degradation of methylene blue dye: Insights into the catalytic mechanism and toxicity assessment

coredata.dc:description: Heterogeneous oxone activation has proved to be a promising technique for the abatement of organic pollutants. Herein, we report the facile synthesis of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite via solvothermal approach utilizing ethylene glycol as solvent and the carbon dots prepared from Pithecellobium dulce arils as the reducing agent for Pd NPs. The as-synthesized composite was characterized and it was then exploited for the methylene blue (MB) degradation via oxone activation. The factors influencing MB degradation such as initial pH, oxone concentration, catalyst dose, initial MB concentration, inorganic ions and temperature were studied. About 94 % of 10 mg/L MB dye could be degraded in 120 min. The synergistic effect of carbon dots and Pd/CeO2 nanoparticles has aided in the enhancement of MB degradation efficiency. The radical scavenging, electrochemical and XPS studies revealed the key roles played by 1O2, O2 •-, e− and SO4 •− in the MB degradation. The toxicity of the formed intermediates were assessed using Ecological Structure Activity Relationship (ECOSAR) programme. The reusability of the catalyst was evaluated. Thus, the present work sheds light on the utility of palladium-based composites in activating oxone to remove organic pollutants present in water streams.

head.ce:title: Unleashing the potential of carbon dots bound Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X as an oxone activator for the degradation of methylene blue dye: Insights into the catalytic mechanism and toxicity assessment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed to be University, Gandhigram 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed to be University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heterogeneous oxone activation has proved to be a promising technique for the abatement of organic pollutants. Herein, we report the facile synthesis of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite via solvothermal approach utilizing ethylene glycol as solvent and the carbon dots prepared from Pithecellobium dulce arils as the reducing agent for Pd NPs. The as-synthesized composite was characterized and it was then exploited for the methylene blue (MB) degradation via oxone activation. The factors influencing MB degradation such as initial pH, oxone concentration, catalyst dose, initial MB concentration, inorganic ions and temperature were studied. About 94 % of 10 mg/L MB dye could be degraded in 120 min. The synergistic effect of carbon dots and Pd/CeO2 nanoparticles has aided in the enhancement of MB degradation efficiency. The radical scavenging, electrochemical and XPS studies revealed the key roles played by 1O2, O2 •-, e− and SO4 •− in the MB degradation. The toxicity of the formed intermediates were assessed using Ecological Structure Activity Relationship (ECOSAR) programme. The reusability of the catalyst was evaluated. Thus, the present work sheds light on the utility of palladium-based composites in activating oxone to remove organic pollutants present in water streams.

Methylene blue dye degradation by oxone activation using Carbon dots assisted Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite occurred via both radical and non-radical pathway. Non-radical pathway was confirmed as the major pathway through radical scavenging, electrochemical and XPS studies.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The textile, and paper industries release large volumes of effluents containing synthetic dyes into water streams, making it unfit for drinking. Methylene blue (MB), a cationic dye mainly used as a coloring agent for paper and fabrics, has been claimed to cause disorders in humans and aquatic organisms. Large doses of MB (> 7.0 mg/kg) can cause nausea, mental disorders, abdominal pain, hypertension etc.[1]. Hence, there is an urgent need to treat effluents containing MB. In the past 20 years, AOPs have garnered attention due to their efficacy in degrading recalcitrant pollutants. AOPs include Fenton reactions, Fenton-based techniques (electro-Fenton, photo-Fenton, Sono-Fenton), photocatalysis, electrochemical oxidation, ozone oxidation etc. are based on the production of reactive species like •OH, SO4 •-, •O2 − respectively [2]. The •OH is a strong oxidant with an oxidation potential of 1.8–2.8 V vs. NHE with a non-selective nature, hence capable of degrading almost all organic moieties. However, it lacks drawbacks of operability in acidic environments, lesser lifetime (~10−9 s), need for large amounts of ferrous salts and H2O2 and difficulty in storage and transport [3–5]. The sulfate radical-based AOPs, owing to their high oxidation potential (E0 = 2.6–3.1 V vs. NHE) and applicability in wide pH range are being utilized in the place of •OH. The sulfate radicals (SO4 •-) has a lifetime of 30–40 μs with high selectivity towards pollutants, making it an exciting candidate for waste-water treatment [6–8]. SO4 •- is usually formed via the decomposition of persulfate (PS) and peroxymonosulfate (PMS) anions. The oxidation potentials of PS (2.01 V) and PMS (1.82 V) are greater than H2O2 (1.76 V) [9]. PMS is widely used for generating SO4 •- although it has a lower oxidation potential than PS. PMS also known as oxone (commercial name), is a triple salt (2KHSO5·KHSO4·K2SO4), a disinfectant with a bleaching nature [10]. Some of the methods used for the activation of oxone and PS are UV irradiation, thermal, electrochemical reactions, piezoelectric, and sonolysis [11]. However, these methods are not explored much owing to their high energy consumption and operational cost. Activation by transition metals (Co2+, Fe2+ , Mn2+) and their oxides such as Fe3O4, Co3O4, MnO2, γ-Fe2O3 has the advantage of simple operation and low cost, but the emergence of secondary pollutants is unavoidable [12–14]. To combat this issue, transition metals can be supported on solid supports such as activated carbon, graphene, clay, zeolite etc. [15]. Zeolites are crystalline microporous alumina silicates with higher thermal and hydrothermal stability, making them an exciting candidate for the decoration of metal, metal oxide nanoparticles and carbon dots. The high surface to volume ratio of the metal oxide nanoparticles and the adsorption capacity of zeolites could enhance the catalytic efficiency [16].

Among the noble metals, palladium can be exploited as an oxone activator due to its capability of inducing catalytic reduction of sulphate salts to sulphate radicals [17]. For instance, Ahn et.al reported that Pd/Al2O3 has outperformed Co2+ and other supported noble metals (Ag/Al2O3, Pt/Al2O3 and Au/Al2O3) in the oxone activation for the oxidative degradation of organic pollutants [18]. Feng et.al had reported the degradation of 1,4-dioxane via oxone activation by Pd/Al2O3 [19]. Wang et.al had supported Pd on graphitic carbon nitride (Pd/g-C3N4) and activated oxone for the degradation of Bisphenol-A [20]. Only few reports are available for the use of Pd based materials in the oxone activation.

Ceria (CeO2) is a non-toxic, inexpensive, and abundant rare earth oxide known for its unique redox properties. The adaptability between two oxidation states (+3 and + 4) and the presence of oxygen vacancies makes it an exciting candidate in AOPs [21]. Due to their larger lattice radius, metals can be doped into the lattice of ceria forming a composite material. Ceria can promote the redox cycle of metals to generate more active sites for pollutant degradation. On the other hand, oxygen vacancies being the sites for the adsorption of oxygen can aid in the production of reactive species such as •O2 − and 1O2 which are vital for the abatement of organic pollutants. Ceria have Ce (III) defects which build up more negative charges on its surface essential for the coordination of metal nanoparticles [22,23]. Ceria has been reported as supports for metals such as CuO, Fe3O4, Co3O4, MnO2, γ-Fe2O3, CdS etc. in the activation of oxone. Ceria supported palladium nanoparticles are well known catalysts in the reactions like methanol oxidation, CO oxidation, hydrogen oxidation, alcohol oxidation, hydrolysis of ammonia borane etc. [24]. Hence, an attempt to utilize Pd/CeO2 NPs in the activation of oxone for the degradation of organic pollutants has been made in the present study. Palladium nanoparticles can be prepared via the reduction of Pd precursors in the presence of reducing agents. Our research group previously exploited nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) obtained from Morinda citrifolia fruit as a reducing agent for the synthesis of Pd NPs and used it as an electrocatalyst for HER [25].

In this context, we herein report the facile synthesis of Pd/CeO2 supported zeolite 13X as an oxone activator for the degradation of methylene blue dye. Carbon dots (CDs), were synthesized using Pithecellobium dulce arils extract via hydrothermal carbonization method and used as the reductant for Pd NPs. The synthesized composite was thoroughly characterized. Moreover, the influence of initial pH, catalyst dosage, oxone concentration, initial MB concentration, inorganic ions and temperature on the MB degradation were investigated. The radical scavenging, EPR, electrochemical and XPS studies were done to decipher the roles of Pd and CeO2 in the oxone activation. Based on the mass fragments, a plausible degradation pathway was proposed. Moreover, the toxicity of MB and its intermediates to aquatic organisms were evaluated. Besides, the reusability of catalyst was also studied.

Palladium chloride, zeolite 13X were procured from Sigma Aldrich, India. Methylene blue, malachite green, methyl orange, and safranin O dyes were purchased from Nice Chemicals, India. Cerium nitrate, oxone, sodium azide, sodium chloride, sodium carbonate, and sodium sulphate were purchased from CDH chemicals, India. The Characterization techniques and electrochemical measurements were discussed in Text S1.

Initially, 5 g of Pithecellobium dulce arils collected from our Institute campus were washed with water and ground in a mixer. Then, about 30 mL of the arils extract was taken in a Teflon container, placed in the steel autoclave and the hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained brown solution was filtered using Whatman No.40 filter paper and centrifuged for 15 min to remove larger particles. The supernatant liquid portion was stored at refrigerator for further use.

Initially, CeO2 nanoparticles were synthesized by co-precipitation method followed by calcination [26]. About 0.1 M cerium nitrate solution was dissolved in 100 mL of double distilled water and aqueous ammonia was added to initiate precipitation. Then the solution was magnetically stirred for 12 h at 70 °C. The white precipitate obtained was washed with ethanol and water several times and dried in oven at 70 °C overnight. Finally, the dried precipitate was calcined in a muffle furnace at 400 °C for 4 h. The CeO2@zeolite 13X composite was prepared via wet-impregnation technique. 2 g zeolite 13X was mixed with 500 mg of the prepared CeO2 NPs and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The obtained precipitate was washed with water twice, followed by drying at 70 °C overnight. Then, the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite was synthesized via solvothermal method. About, 10 mL CDs solution was mixed with 30 mL ethylene glycol and then about 1 g of CeO2@zeolite 13X was added to the above solution and sonicated for 30 min to obtain homogenous mixture. Afterwards, 0.01 M PdCl2 solution was added to the above mixture and stirred for 10 min and was transferred to a Teflon container and placed in a steel autoclave at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling, black precipitate obtained was washed thoroughly with ethanol and water thrice and dried in oven at 75 °C. Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of synthesis of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite.

The oxone activation-degradation reactions were carried out in a temperature controlled orbital shakers under dark condition. Methylene blue was chosen as the target pollutant. About 100 mL of MB solution was taken in the 150 mL glass reactor and the pH was adjusted to desired values with 0.1 M HCl or 0.1 M NaOH. The temperature was kept at 30 ± 2 °C. The catalyst was added to the reactor and the solution was under stirring for 30 min to establish adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Soon after adsorption process, certain amount of oxone was added to initiate the degradation. At the predetermined time intervals, 3.0 mL of sample was withdrawn and immediately quenched with excess of methanol to stop further degradation reaction. The solution was analyzed for the change in its absorption intensity at absorption maximum of 665 nm. The influence of operating parameters such as initial pH, initial MB concentration, oxone concentration, catalyst dose, inorganic ions and temperature were studied. To identify the reactive species involved in MB degradation: ethanol, tertiary butanol, ascorbic acid, and sodium azide were taken as scavengers. The toxicity of the formed intermediates was evaluated using Ecological Structure Activity Relationships (ECOSAR) software developed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). To further assess the toxicity of the formed intermediates, bacterial viability test was performed using spread plate technique [27]. Briefly 0.1 mL of parent MB and solution after 120 min degradation was spread on the Nutrient agar plate separately. Then, the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After 24 h of incubation, colonies were counted and recorded. To evaluate the reusability of the catalyst, it was recovered after the degradation reaction through centrifugation and then washed thrice with water, dried and used as catalyst for 5 cycles. All the reactions were conducted twice, and the values were plotted as mean values ± standard deviations.

The UV–visible spectrum of Pithecellobium dulce arils extract shown in Fig. 2a showed a sharp peak at 268 nm and a broad peak at 315 nm characteristic of ᴨ-ᴨ* and n-ᴨ* transition of the polyphenols present in the extract. The synthesized CDs exhibited a sharp peak at 280 nm related to the ᴨ-ᴨ* transition of the CC (sp2) moieties present in the CDs [28]. Fig. 2b is the maximum excitation and emission spectrum of CDs. When excited at 340 nm, a maximum PL emission occurred at 426 nm and the maximum excitation peak was mirror symmetric to maximum emission peak. The photographic images of the synthesized CDs in daylight and UV light (365 nm) is shown in inset of Fig. 2b which revealed the emission of blue fluorescence by the CDs. The PL emission spectra obtained at varied excitation wavelengths is displayed in Fig. 2c. It could be noted that the intensity of PL emission increased when excitation wavelength was increased from 290 to 340 nm which could be due to the ᴨ-ᴨ* transitions of the carbon cores of graphite [29,30]. When the excitation wavelength was increased to 400 nm, the emission spectra of CDs shifted to higher wavelengths with decreased intensities. It was clear that the CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior which can be due to the varied sized CDs and emissive trap states on the CDs surface [31].

Fig. 2d shows the XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs. The diffraction pattern showed a sharp peak at 24.890 and a weak peak at 42.40 corresponding to (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon respectively[32]. The broad peak was due to the smaller size of CDs[33]. The FT-IR spectrum showed in Fig. 2e revealed the presence of C-OH (3194 cm−1), CO (1697 cm−1), CC (1605 cm−1), CH2 (1476 cm−1), C-O-C (1310 cm−1), C-OH (1190 cm−1), and CO (998 cm−1) functional groups present in CDs surface. The presence of oxygen containing functional groups enhances the hydrophilicity of CDs [34]. Fig. 2f illustrates the TEM image obtained for CDs aqueous solution. It revealed the well-dispersed spherically shaped CDs particles. The particle size distribution chart (Fig. 2g) showed that the particle size of CDs were in the range of 2–6 nm and the mean particle size was identified as 4.23 ± 0.23 nm. The HR-TEM image displayed in Fig. 2h revealed the lattice fringe of 0.215 nm which was in accordance with the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [35]. The diffused ring pattern observed in SAED pattern (Fig. 2i) revealed the amorphous nature of CDs.

The optical properties of the as-synthesized CeO2 NPs, CeO2@Zeolite 13X and Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite were studied using UV–visible spectroscopy. The UV–visible spectra and the corresponding Tauc plots for CeO2 NPs, CeO2@zeolite 13X and Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite are displayed in Fig. 3a, b. It was evident from the spectra that the synthesized materials exhibited strong absorption in the UV region which was due to the charge-transfer transitions between O 2p and Ce 4f states [36].The absorption edge of CeO2 NPs was found to be 520 nm but it was blue shifted to 460 nm in the case of CeO2@zeolite 13X composite which might be due to the quantum confinement effects [37]. For the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite, the absorption edge extended to higher wavelengths i.e. visible light region which highlights the pivotal role of Pd NPs. The direct band gap of the synthesized materials were calculated using the relation (1).
(1)
αh ν
2
= K
hν −
E g
where α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck's constant (6.626 × 10−34 J s), K is a proportionality constant and ν is the frequency of photons. It could be seen from the Fig. 3b that the Pd/CeO2 @zeolite 13X composite has a narrower band gap value of 2.30 eV than CeO2 NPs (2.75 eV) and CeO2@zeolite 13X composite (2.79 eV). Thus, Pd has created additional energy levels between the valence and conduction bands of CeO2 which resulted in the narrowing of band gap [38].

The FTIR spectrum of as synthesized CeO2 NPs displayed in Fig. 3c showed bands at 3414, and 1635 cm−1 which corresponds to stretching and bending vibrations of OH groups. The bands at 1738, 1217, and 451 cm−1 corresponds to the CO, CO, and CeO respectively. The band at 1372 cm−1 corresponds to the O-C-O stretching frequency of carbonate species formed due to the interaction of lattice oxygen present in CeO2 NPs with atmospheric CO2. The bands in the range of 1000–800 cm−1 were due to the presence of residual organic or carbonate species on the ceria surface. The band at 726 cm−1 was attributed to the envelope of the phonon band of ceria network [39,40].

The FTIR spectra of the synthesized nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3d. The broad band at 3310 cm−1 and 1644 cm−1 was attributed to the presence of water molecules in the zeolite moiety and the bending vibration of water molecules. The band at 1402 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching vibrations of carbonate species. The bands centered at 993 and 675 cm−1 were due to the tetrahedral symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of SiO4 or AlO4. The bands at 549 cm−1 and 468 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibrations of octahedral structures and internal SiO4 or AlO4 tetrahedral units. A sharp band at 437 cm−1 belongs to M–O (M = Si or Al) internal bending vibrations [41]. For Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite, the bands corresponding to zeolite 13X were blue shifted with decreased intensity which indicated the interactions of Pd/CeO2 NPs with zeolite 13X surface. Two additional bands were observed at 1736 and 1224 cm−1 which might be due to the CO and CO stretching frequency of CDs [34].

Fig. 4a shows the XRD spectrum of CeO2 NPs. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.54, 33.15, 47.66, 56.3, 59, 69.7, 77.2, 79, and 88.270 corresponding to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331), (420), and (422) planes respectively were indexed to cubic fluorite structure of CeO2 (JCPDS 43–1002) [42].The average crystallite size of CeO2 was calculated using Debye-Scherrer equation shown in eq. 2 and it was estimated to be 3.20 nm.
(2)
D =
Kλ
βCos θ
where D, K, λ, β and θ represents the average crystallite size, Scherer constant value of 0.9, wavelength of X-ray radiation (0.154 nm), FWHM in radians and the Bragg's angle. The diffraction peaks related to CeO2 were seen in CeO2@zeolite 13X composite with increased crystallinity (Fig. 4b). However, peaks due to zeolite 13X was not detected which might be due to the dense coverage of CeO2 NPs over the zeolite 13X surface which attributed to the amorphization of zeolite 13X [43,44]. The XRD spectrum of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite shown in Fig. 4c revealed the diffraction peaks of zeolite 13X which meant that the decoration of Pd NPs over CeO2@zeolite 13X has enhanced the crystallinity of zeolite 13X during solvothermal process. The peaks at 2θ = 40.03, 46.4, 68.2, 81.78, and 86.30 were indexed to FCC structured Pd NPs (JCPDS 882335). The diffraction peaks of CeO2 NPs were also seen but with decreased intensity.

The morphology of the synthesized composites can be identified using SEM analysis. The irregularly shaped CeO2 nanoparticles with predominant spherical aggregates are revealed by the SEM image displayed in Fig. 5a. The SEM image of the zeolite 13X showed the ordered octahedral particles (Fig. 5b). The SEM images of CeO2@zeolite 13X composites are shown in Fig. 5c and d which revealed the dense coverage of ceria nanoparticles over the octahedral shaped zeolite 13X particles. The zeolite 13X particles were found to be irregular and less ordered. Fig. 5e-g revealed the decoration of smaller sized spherical Pd/CeO2 NPs over the ordered octahedral zeolite 13X particles. To further identify the presence of Pd/CeO2 NPs over zeolite 13X surface, elemental mapping analysis was done and the corresponding images are shown in Fig. 5h-n. The elemental mapping images revealed the presence of elements C, O, Na, Al, Si, Pd and Ce. It also revealed the presence of CDs in the PdCeO2@zeolite 13X composite. The EDX spectra of CeO2@zeolite 13X and PdCeO2@zeolite 13X composites are displayed in Fig. S1 which also revealed the presence of aforementioned elements. The weight % of the elements are shown in Table 1 . Thus, the results confirmed the successful preparation of CeO2@zeolite 13X and PdCeO2@zeolite 13X composites.

The size and morphology of the prepared composite can be analyzed using HR-TEM studies. Fig. 6a shows the agglomerated spherically shaped Pd/CeO2 NPs embedded in the zeolite 13X surface. The lattice fringes of 0.31, 0.27, 0.23, 0.13 nm corresponding to (111), (100) planes of fluorite structured CeO2 and (111), (220) planes of FCC structured Pd NPs were identified from Fig. 6b. The particle size distribution chart shown in Fig. 6c revealed the mean particle size of Pd/CeO2 NPs as 9.37 ± 0.25 nm. The SAED pattern showed in Fig. 6d revealed the polycrystallinity of the prepared Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite and it also depicted the (100) plane of graphitic carbon of CDs in addition to (220) and (222) planes of Pd NPs.

Fig. 6e illustrates the TGA spectrum of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite. The weight loss of the synthesized composite occurred in three regions accounting for 31.26 % of total weight loss. The first weight loss of about 8.81 % in the range of 30–100 °C was due to the evaporation of adsorbed water molecules while the second and third weight loss of about 11.90 % and 10.55 % occurred within 100–250 °C and 250–800 °C respectively which might be due to the removal of organic species [41]. Thus, the TGA analysis depicted the thermal stability of the synthesized Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite. The textural properties of the synthesized Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite was evaluated using BET analysis. Fig. 6f shows that the synthesized nanocomposite exhibited Type-IV adsorption-desorption isotherm of IUPAC classification with H3 hysteresis loop characteristic of mesoporous materials. The BET specific surface area of zeolite 13X was reported as 615.5 m2/g, [45] but after the decoration of Pd/CeO2 NPs there was a drastic decrease in SBET of 13.626 m2/g which was evident of the occupation of CDs/Pd/CeO2 NPs in the pores of zeolite 13X [46]. The BJH pore size distribution curve displayed in Fig. 6g also revealed the presence of mesopores with the diameter of 3.638 nm and pore volume of 0.025 cc/g.

To further identify the chemical composition and valence states of the elements present in the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite, XPS analysis was done. Fig. 7a displayed the survey scan spectrum of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite. It further supported the results obtained from EDX. The C 1s core level was deconvoluted into peaks centered at 284.81 eV (C-C/C=C), 286.17 eV (CO), 288.8 eV (C=O), and 293.6 eV (ᴨ- ᴨ*) [31,47,48] (Fig. 7b).The identified peaks were related to C1s peaks of CDs, which corroborates the HR-TEM results. Therefore, it is likely that CDs could be present on the surface or inside the pores of the zeolite.

The O 1s core level peak at 530 eV was deconvoluted into four peaks (Fig. 7c). The peak at 530.05 eV corresponds to the surface lattice oxygen (CeO) while the peak centered at 531.2 eV was due to the CO functional group of CDs. The peaks at 532.15 and 533.29 eV were attributed to the surface adsorbed oxygen and C-O-C bond of CDs [49]. The relative concentrations of lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen were calculated as 3.18 % and 52.45 % respectively. The oxygen vacancies present in the PdCeO2@zeolite 13X composite can be calculated using the expression (Oads/ Olattice + Oads). The greater the value, greater will be the oxygen vacancy. From this expression, the oxygen vacancy was measured to be 0.94 which confirmed that the synthesized Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite consists of abundant oxygen vacancies which would be beneficial for adsorption and activation of oxygen vital for oxidation reactions. It was also reported that Oads showed higher activity in oxidation reactions [50].

The deconvoluted Pd 3d spectrum is shown in Fig. 7d. A doublet at 335.4 eV and 340.7 eV corresponds to Pd (0) in 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 levels. Another doublet at 337 eV and 342.2 eV was due to Pd (II) in 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 levels. The relative concentration of Pd (0) and Pd (II) was 81.96 %, 18.04 % respectively [25,51]. It confirmed that Pd is present exclusively in (0) oxidation state. The Ce 3d spectrum was deconvoluted into several peaks (Fig. 7e). The peaks at binding energies 886 eV and 904 eV were ascribed to Ce3+ species of Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 levels while peaks at binding energies 882 eV and 902 eV were due to Ce4+ species Ce of 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 levels. Satellite peaks of Ce4+ appeared at 898 eV and 917 eV [52]. The relative concentrations of Ce3+ and Ce4+ were found to be 52.24, 45.29 % respectively. The % atomic concentrations of C, O, Pd, and Ce were found to be 36.98, 60.78, 0.99, and 1.25 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of catalytic efficiency of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the MB removal in different systems, MB degradation studies were carried out in oxone alone, CDs/oxone, CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone and Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone systems. It can be seen from Fig. 8a. that oxone alone cannot remove MB efficiently since the removal efficiency was only 26.01 % within 2 h which was due to the lower oxidation potential of oxone (1.82 V) [9]. Since CDs were present in Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite which was evident from SEM-EDX, HR-TEM, and BET results, it was also employed as a catalyst. However, CDs/oxone can remove only 37.2 % of MB. When CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone was used a catalyst, about 69.19 % of MB could be removed. It was mainly attributed to the adsorption process since 62 % of MB was removed within 30 min of adsorption. While, 94 % of MB was removed within 2 h by Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone system in which 44 % removal occurred during adsorption process. To further evaluate the role of Pd in the oxone activation, MB degradation study was carried out using CeO2@zeolite 13X along with CDs as catalyst. Only 70 % of MB was degraded in 120 min which showed that the addition of CDs has not enhanced the degradation efficiency of CeO2@zeolite 13X. Therefore, Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X was found to be superior over other materials.

The MB removal by different systems followed pseudo-first order kinetics and the corresponding rate constants are shown in Fig. 8b. The rate constants, k followed the order: Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone (0.0246 min−1) > CeO2@zeolite 13X/CDs/oxone (0.0062 min−1) > CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone (0.0055 min−1) > CDs/oxone (0.0032 min−1) > oxone (0.0017 min−1). Thus, it was evident that the synergistic effect of CDs and Pd/ CeO2 NPs has significantly enhanced the degradation efficiency of MB through oxone activation, and hence Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone system was chosen for the degradation of MB. The comparative results are presented in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of operational parameters on MB removal

ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the initial pH of the solution on the MB degradation was investigated by varying pH from 4 to 9 and the concentration vs. time plot is shown in Fig. 9a and their rate constants are shown in Fig. 9b. It is inferred that the removal efficiency increased on increasing pH from 4 to 7. At pH = 4, the removal efficiency was only 48.7 % while at neutral pH, there is a drastic increase in removal efficiency i.e. 94 % which could be understood based on the point of zero charge (pHzc) of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X and the dominant oxone species at specific pH [53]. The pHzc of the synthesized composite was calculated as 6.22. The surface of Pd/CeO2@Zeolite 13X at pH < 6.22 will be positively charged which is not favorable for the adsorption of MB as it is a cationic dye. But the formation of SO4 •- is favorable at acidic pH however, the self-scavenging of SO4 •- is in equal proportion hence lesser removal efficiency. At pH > 6.22, the catalyst surface becomes negatively charged, therefore electrostatic attraction between positively charged MB molecules and negatively charged catalyst surface enhanced the removal efficiency. It was reported that oxone exists predominantly as HSO5 − at pH = 6–8 and SO5 2− at pH > 11[54]. HSO5 − is the active species to generate SO4 •- while SO5 2− shows lower activity than HSO5 − . Hence, at pH = 7, about 94 % MB was removed with the rate constant of 0.0246 min−1. When the pH was raised to 9, a decline in removal efficiency (88 %) was observed. Therefore, neutral pH (pH = 7) was chosen for the MB degradation study.

The influence of various Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X doses such as 0.10, 0.25, 0.4, and 0.55 g/L on MB degradation was studied and shown in Fig. 9c, d. The % MB removal and the corresponding rate constants had significantly increased from 44.2 % to 95 % on increasing the dosage from 0.10 to 0.55 g/L. It denoted the generation of more active sites on the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X surface, which facilitated the oxone activation resulting in the generation of reactive species for MB degradation [55]. Further, the MB degradation efficiency was quite similar for 0.40 and 0.55 g/L. Hence, 0.40 g/L of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X was enough for efficient MB degradation via oxone activation.

To elucidate the influence of the oxone concentration on the MB degradation, different oxone concentrations ranging from 1.5 mM–7.5 mM was used. From the Fig. 9e, it could be seen, the % removal of MB was 67.3 %, 76 %, and 94 % when oxone concentration was set at 1.5 mM, 2.5 mM, and 5.0 mM respectively. It indicated the enhancement of MB degradation when higher oxone concentrations were used. However, when the oxone concentration was further increased to 7.5 mM, the % removal increased during first 20 min, but afterwards it decreased gradually. It was due to the fact that excessive oxone would inhibit the degradation due to the reaction between HSO5 − and reactive oxygen species [56]. The corresponding rate constants are displayed in Fig. 9f. On the basis of the results of the study, the optimum oxone concentration for the present study was chosen as 5.0 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of initial concentration of MB
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of initial concentration of MB dyes was studied by varying initial concentrations (5, 10, 20 and 30 ppm) and keeping initial pH, catalyst dose, and oxone concentration constant. On increasing the MB concentration, the removal efficiency was found to be decreased from 98 % (5 ppm) to 72.27 % (30 ppm) within 2 h (Fig. 9g) and the corresponding rate constants also declined in the same order as above i.e. 0.0354 min−1 to 0.0088 min−1 (Fig. 9h). The constant production of SO4 •- /•OH by oxone at fixed concentrations could be the reason for the decline in reactivity. At higher MB concentrations, more number of MB dye molecules may compete for SO4 •- /•OH and the unavailability of SO4 •- /•OH would result in lower removal rates. In addition, the contact between oxone and the active sites of catalyst will be lost when more number of MB dye molecules were present in the solution as they compete for active sites via adsorption [57]. Also, higher the concentration of MB, more time will be taken by the catalyst to completely remove the MB molecules [58]. Hence lower removal efficiency was seen at higher MB concentrations.

Another important factor influencing MB degradation is the temperature. Five different temperatures (25, 30, 35, 40, and 45 °C) were chosen for the study and the corresponding degradation plots are shown in Fig. S2a. It was obvious that the degradation efficiency enhances when temperature rises which was due to the fact that heat energy facilitates the breakage of OO bond in oxone promoting the generation of radical species, which was beneficial for MB degradation [59]. Also, the collision between catalyst and MB molecules are higher at elevated temperatures, which would enhance the reactivity of the system [60]. The activation energy (Ea) of MB degradation was calculated using Arrhenius equation (eq. 3)
(3)
lnk = lnA −
E a
RT
where Ea, k, A, R, and T denotes the activation energy (kJ mol−1), rate constant (min−1), pre-exponential factor, universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1) and absolute temperature (K) respectively. It was reported that surface-controlled reactions have higher activation energies (>29 kJ mol−1) and diffusion-controlled reactions have lower activation energies i.e.8–21 kJ mol−1 [61]. The corresponding Arrhenius plot is displayed in Fig. S2b and from the obtained Ea value of 16.091 kJ mol−1, it could be concluded that MB degradation is a diffusion-controlled process.

Inorganic ions are omnipresent in real waste waters and has the tendency to interfere with the generated ROSs leading to the loss of its oxidizing nature and it also influence the self-decomposition of oxone. Therefore, it is essential to evaluate the influence of inorganic anions on the oxone activation. In the present work, the influence of Cl−, SO4 2−, and CO3 2− ions on the oxone activation for MB degradation were studied (Fig. S2c, d). Compared to the MB degradation in the absence of inorganic ions, no significant changes has occurred in the presence of 5 mM CO3 2−and SO4 2− ions. However, when 5 mM Cl− ions was taken, the degradation efficiency has increased slightly from 94 % to 96.12 %. It was reported that Cl− ions react with HSO5 − and SO4 •− to form active chlorine species such as Cl2, Cl•, and HOCl, which acts as secondary oxidants thereby enhancing the MB degradation [62]. Also, the generated active chlorine species would attack the unsaturated bonds of MB forming organic halides which gets adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst, favorable for the MB degradation [63]. Thus, the inorganic anions were found to be less interfering in the MB degradation which suggests the possibility of non-radical pathway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Universality of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone system to other organic dyes
ce:section.ce:para: To further identify the universality of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone system to other organic dyes, the degradation study of methyl orange (MO), safranin O (SO), and malachite green (MG) was carried out under the optimum conditions as reported previously for MB degradation i.e. (pH = 7.0, oxone = 5.0 mM, Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X = 0.40 g/L, [Dye]0 = 10 ppm). Fig. S2e, f displayed the degradation plots and the corresponding rate constants. It could be seen from the Fig. S2e that about 88 %, 81.5 % and 98 % of MO, SO, and MG dyes were removed within 2 h. The MO and SO dyes showed little resistance to oxone activation while MG dye could be removed efficiently by Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone system. It proved the universality of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone system to wide range of organic dyes. The comparison of previously reported Pd and Ce-based catalysts with Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite is displayed in Table S1 and S2.

The reusability and stability of the catalysts were essential to take up this method to community level. For this purpose, the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite was collected through centrifugation and washed with water and dried in oven. It was reused for five consecutive cycles and the corresponding degradation plots are shown in Fig. S3a. After five cycles, the MB degradation efficiency of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composites was decreased only by 4.8 % which demonstrated the high stability of the composite. The decline in MB degradation after reuse was due to the loss of active sites of catalyst due to the adsorption of MB intermediates [64]. The FT-IR spectrum and SEM images of the catalyst after fifth run are shown in Fig. S3b Compared with the fresh Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X, red shift in wavenumbers were observed and a band at 1736 cm−1 disappeared which might be due to the dissolution of CDs during repeated study. A weak band newly appeared at 1327 cm−1 attributed to the stretching frequency of SO bond corresponding to either HSO5 − or SO4 2− which confirmed the interaction of oxone with Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X [65]. The SEM image shown in Fig. S3c, revealed a slight change in morphology from smooth to rough surfaces after five cycles. To further support the obtained results, BET analysis was carried out for the recycled Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X catalyst and the resultant adsorption-desorption isotherm is presented in Fig. S3d. The recycled Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X catalyst after fifth run also exhibited Type-IV adsorption-desorption isotherm with H3 hysteresis loop characteristic of mesoporous materials. The BET specific surface area was calculated as 54.15 m2/g which was greater than that of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X before degradation (13.626 m2/g). It implies the dissolution of CDs from the pores of zeolite 13X which is in accordance with the FT-IR result. The BJH pore size distribution curve revealed the presence of mesopores with the average pore diameter of 6.96 nm and pore volume of 0.1341 cc/g (Fig. S3e).

The MB degradation via oxone activation by various heterogeneous transition metal catalyst has been reported earlier and claimed to have occurred through radical mediated (•OH, SO4 •−, O2 •-) and non-radical mediated (1O2, electron transfer between oxone and pollutants) degradation pathways [10,66].To get deep insight into the reaction mechanism, radical scavenging tests were carried out to identify the reactive oxygen species generated during the activation of oxone by Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X. The radical scavengers such as EtOH, TBA, ascorbic acid and NaN3 were used to scavenge •OH, SO4 •−, O2 •- , and 1O2 respectively. EtOH can be used as a scavenger for both •OH, and SO4 •− with a rate constant of 9.1 × 106 M−1 s−1 and 3.5 × 107 M−1 s−1.TBA is exclusively used as •OH scavenger (k = 3.8–7.6 × 108 M−1 s−1) [67].The degradation plots and the corresponding rate constants are shown in Fig. 10a,b. In the presence of 20 mM EtOH, the degradation efficiency was decreased to 90 % while 20 mM TBA addition was found to be ineffective in quenching the reaction. From the results, it can be inferred that SO4 •− played a minor role while •OH had no effects. In order to identify the presence of O2 •- and 1O2, 20 mM ascorbic acid and NaN3 (k = 1 × 108 M−1 s−1) were added to the Pd/CeO2@Zeolite 13X/oxone/MB system and it can be seen that in the presence of ascorbic acid and NaN3, the removal efficiency decreased to 59 % and 44 % respectively. The k values decreased from 0.0246 min−1 to 0.0048 min−1 and 0.0015 min−1 respectively, which confirmed the generation of O2 •- , and 1O2 and hence, it was clear that MB degradation followed both radical and non-radical mechanistic pathways. To further prove the results, MB degradation was carried out in oxygen-free conditions (N2 purging) to rule out O2 •- generation through oxygen dissolved in solution. Fig. S4 shows that the degradation efficiency hasn't declined in anaerobic conditions which proves that generation of O2 •- is not occurred via reduction of dissolved oxygen but through the surface adsorbed oxygen. Phenol can be used as the scavenger for •OH, SO4 •−, and 1O2 whose rate constants are 6.6 × 109 M−1 s−1, 8.8 × 109 M−1 s−1, and 2.3 × 106 M−1 s−1 [68]. In the presence of 20 mM phenol (Fig.S4), only 53 % of MB could be degraded with the rate constant of 0.0029 min−1. Since, the removal efficiency is lower than that of EtOH and TBA quenching, it is confirmed that 1O2 is the major reactive species. The disadvantages of radical mechanism has been reported by researchers claiming that •OH, and SO4 •− could be hindered by the inorganic ions/organic matter present in the water system leading to the trapping of reactive oxygen species, hence lower degradation efficiency was observed [69]. However, non-radical mechanism mediated by 1O2 are less affected by the presence of interference ions, therefore it demonstrated higher degradation efficiency [70]. Thus, radical scavenging studies confirmed that 1O2 played a significant role in the MB degradation. EPR analysis was done for Pd/CeO2@Zeolite 13X to ascertain the presence of oxygen vacancies. EPR spectra of Pd/CeO2@Zeolite 13X is shown in Fig. 10c. A broad signal with a
g ⊥
= 2.142 and a sharp signal at
g ⊥
= 1.99 was due to the Ce3+ ions and quasi-free electrons trapped in the oxygen vacancies [71]. A weaker signal with
g ∥
=4.035 might be due to the presence of oxygen adsorbed on the Ce3+ defective sites [72]. Higher the intensity of the EPR signal, greater will be the oxygen vacancies in the synthesized composite [73].Hence, it is confirmed that Pd/CeO2@Zeolite 13X composite has more oxygen vacancies conducive for the generation of ROS.

On the other hand, electrochemical analysis was carried out to investigate the contribution of electron transfer in the degradation of MB. It was verified by open-circuit potential (OCP) studies (Fig. 10d). The initial potential of the Pd/CeO2@Zeolite 13X after the addition of oxone increased from 0.304 V to 0.4498 V which confirmed the generation of metastable oxone/ Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X surface complex. After the addition of MB, the potential slightly decreased followed by gradual increase which suggested electron transfer from MB to metastable oxone/ Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X surface complex [74]. For the identification of interaction between different components, OCP studies were also carried out for CeO2@zeolite 13X composite and CDs. It could be seen from the plots, that after addition of oxone, there is a gradual increase in potentials from 0.263 V to 0.35 V (CeO2@zeolite 13X composite) and 0.1740 V to 0.215 V (CDs), which confirms the generation of metastable oxone/ catalyst surface complex in both the materials. However, the potential is found to be higher for Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X, highlighting the pivotal role of Pd in promoting electron transfer to oxone. After injection of MB, there is also a slight decline in potential as observed in Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X suggesting the electron transfer. The electron transfer process was further verified by chronoamperometry study (Fig. 10e). It could be seen that in the case of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X when oxone was added to the electrolyte after 50 s, there was a sudden decline in the current response. The decrease in the current might be due to the consumption of oxone which indicated the formation of surface bound radicals by the interactions between catalyst and oxone [75]. While after addition of MB to the above system i.e. after ~150 s, there is a slight decrease in current which could be the result of electron transfer from MB to oxone via Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X (mediator). Similarly, the chronoamperometric curves of CeO2@zeolite 13X composite and CDs showed decline in current after injection of oxone. However, the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X showed greater current decline than other two materials which implies larger electron transfer from the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X to oxone [76]. Also, the addition of MB didn't cause prominent decline in current, which again proved the role of Pd in the electron transfer process. Thus, the OCP and chronoamperometry studies revealed the electron-transfer mediated MB degradation promoted majorly by Pd.

XPS spectra of fresh and used Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite could be compared to identify the changes in the chemical compositions after oxone activation and degradation of MB. Fig. 10f-i showed the XPS spectra of the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite after MB degradation. The survey scan spectra displayed in Fig.S5a revealed the presence of elements C, O, Na, Al, Si, Pd and Ce. The C 1s core level was deconvoluted to four peaks similar to that of fresh catalyst with a slight shift in binding energies (Fig. 10f). The relative concentrations of 284.28, 285.30, 288.22, and 293.25 eV were found to be 38.03 %, 52.51 %, 8.14 %, and 1.32 % respectively. The decrease in the concentration of 288.22 eV from 15.10 % to 8.14 % could be due to the participation of CO functional groups in the activation of oxone to generate 1O2 [69]. The O 1s core level spectrum obtained after MB degradation was deconvoluted to four peaks centered at 529.18, 530.5, 531.71, and 532.81 eV respectively (Fig. 10g). The peaks were blue shifted to lower binding energies. Further, the oxygen vacancies had decreased from 0.94 to 0.68 which confirms the participation of oxygen vacancies in the generation of 1O2 [77].

The Pd 3d core levels were deconvoluted into five peaks centered at binding energy of 334.9, 337, 340, 342.6, and 347.5 eV respectively (Fig. 10h). The new peak appeared at 347.5 eV which was reported as energy loss peak corresponding to Pd(II) ions [78]. The relative concentrations of Pd (0) and Pd (II) after MB degradation were found to be 56.16 % and 43.84 % respectively. It could be seen that the concentration of Pd (0) had decreased from 81.96 % to 56.16 % owing to the oxidation of Pd (0) species which confirmed the successful electron transfer from the catalyst to oxone. The Ce 3d core levels were deconvoluted to six different peaks which was similar to the Ce 3d core level before MB degradation (Fig. 10i). The relative concentrations of Ce3+ and Ce4+ changed from 52.24 %, 45.29 % to 40.78 % and 59.23 % which confirmed the redox cycling of Ce3+/Ce4+ ions [59].To elucidate the role played by Ce ions during oxone activation, XPS deconvoluted spectra of CeO2@zeolite 13X composite after MB degradation is presented in Fig. 10j, k. The survey scan spectra revealed the presence of C, N, O, S, Al, Si, Ce (Fig. S5b) which conformed the adsorption of MB over the surface of CeO2@zeolite 13X composite. The O 1s core levels were deconvoluted to two peaks corresponding to 531.5 eV (surface adsorbed oxygen) and 529.43 eV (surface lattice oxygen, CeO) respectively. The oxygen vacancy was calculated as 0.84 which was greater than that of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite after use. It revealed that only adsorption process was predominant in CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone/MB system than non-radical pathway. The Ce 3d core levels were deconvoluted to six peaks similar to that of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X composite but with slight change in intensities. The relative concentrations of Ce3+ and Ce4+ were found to be 29.83 % and 70.16 % which confirmed that Ce3+/Ce4+ ions has involved in the oxone activation [79].Thus, it could be concluded that after the addition of Pd NPs to the CeO2@zeolite 13X composite in the presence of CDs, both radical and non-radical pathways has occurred during oxone activation-MB degradation process.

Based on the aforementioned results, a plausible mechanistic pathway is presented. Initially, adsorption of oxone and MB dye on the surface of Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X occurs simultaneously which is confirmed via FT-IR and XPS studies. The Ce3+ ions activate HSO5 − ions to SO4 •-,OH•, and Ce 4+ ions (eq. 4). The Ce3+ is regenerated along with SO5 •- /H+ by the HSO5 − ions (eq. 5). Pd NPs activates HSO5 − ions to SO4 •- (eq. 6). The redox potential of Ce(IV)/Ce(III) is 1.44 VNHE while that of Pd(II)/Pd(0) is 0.915 VNHE [80]. Therefore, the electron transfer is thermodynamically favorable from Pd (0) to Ce (IV) thereby the redox cycling is continued to yield SO4 •- (eq. 7). The oxygen adsorbed on the oxygen vacancy sites produces O2 •- (eq. 8) [81]. The radical scavenging, EPR, electrochemical and XPS studies confirmed that Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone/MB followed both radical and non-radical pathways but 1O2 played a major role. Since, 1O2 is identified as the primary ROS in MB degradation according to the scavenging experiments, the generation of 1O2 was believed to occur through these possible reactions (a) oxidation of O2 •- by Ce (IV) ions since the redox potential of (O2 •- /1O2) is −0.34 VNHE (eq. 9) [82](b) self-decomposition of oxone in which oxygen vacancies acted as a mediator in lowering the energy barrier of oxone (eq. 10) [83](c) self-reaction of SO5 •- catalysed by the carbonyl groups present in the CDs (eq. 11) [81,84,85] Finally 1O2, O2 •- , SO4 •- degrades and mineralizes MB to CO2 and H2O (eq. 12).

HR-MS was employed to identify the intermediates formed during the degradation of MB by Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone system. A plausible MB degradation pathway was proposed based on the intermediates formed and is shown in Fig. S6. The peak at m/z 284 corresponds to MB molecule. The attack of MB molecule by 1O2 resulted in m/z = 298 [86]. The mass fragments at m/z 270, 256, 228 were due to the demethylation of amino groups. The oxidation-addition reaction of 1O2 was evident from the presence of intermediates at m/z = 302, 318 [87]. The demethylation of m/z = 302 resulted in m/z = 288. The mass fragments at m/z = 274, 246 were formed by the demethylation reaction of m/z = 288. The ring opening of phenothiazine by the desulfurization reaction yielded m/z = 256 and the cleavage of CN group present in m/z = 256 resulted in m/z = 136, 98 [88]. The intermediate m/z = 218 was formed via the deamination reaction. In addition, N and S sites of m/z = 218 were attacked to generate m/z = 172 which further oxidized to m/z = 110 and m/z = 142 [89]. Finally, the intermediates were further oxidized to small molecules such as NO3 −, SO4 2−, H2O and CO2. Total organic carbon (TOC) study was carried out to determine the mineralization of MB. From the Fig. S7, it could be inferred that about 52 % of MB could be mineralized/removed after 120 min degradation which suggests the presence of organic intermediates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity assessment of MB and its intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: The intermediates formed after degradation could be more harmful than parent compound, and hence toxicity assessment need to be done to evaluate the acute/chronic toxicity of intermediates to aquatic organisms. ECOSAR program was utilized to investigate the toxicities of MB and its intermediates to Fish, Daphnid, and Green algae since it avoids the use of animals for toxicity experiments. Table 3 displays the toxicity assessment of MB and its intermediates. The LC50 is referred as the concentration of the substance that kills 50 % of the test animals and EC50 is the concentration of the substance required to obtain 50 % effect [90]. MB was found to be toxic to Fish (5.79 mg/L), Daphnid (3.88 mg/L) and Green algae (5.72 mg/L). However, after oxone activation and degradation, the formed intermediates exerted less toxicity than parent MB and were less harmful. The intermediates m/z = 172, 142 were less harmful among other MB intermediates while m/z = 256,228, and 98 were the toxic intermediates. The bacterial counts for parent MB and solution after 120 min degradation was found to be 7.0 and 23.0 CFU/mL (Fig.S8). It revealed that after oxone activation-degradation, the toxicity of MB has been reduced to certain extent thus improving bacterial growth. However, further studies should be done to eliminate the toxicity of MB.

To sum up, a facile green synthesis of palladium decorated ceria nanoparticles supported zeolite 13X composite (Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X) was reported. CDs prepared from Pithecellobium dulce arils was exploited as green reducing agent. The synthesized composite was characterized using UV–vis, FT-IR, Raman, XRD, SEM-EDX, HR-TEM, TGA, BET, and XPS. Then, the composite was exploited as an oxone activator for methylene blue degradation. The MB degradation efficiency could reach 94 % within 2 h under optimum reaction conditions such as pH = 7.0, catalyst dosage = 0.4g/L, and oxone concentration = 5.0 mM. From the radical scavenging studies, 1O2 was identified as major reactive oxygen species in the Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X/oxone/MB system while O2 •-, e−, and SO4 •- also contributed to the MB degradation. Besides, Pd/CeO2@zeolite 13X could retain 89 % degradation efficiency after five runs, demonstrating its stability. The ECOSAR program revealed the less toxic nature of formed intermediates compared to MB itself. Thus, this work demonstrated the use of Pd based catalytic systems as an alternative catalyst in the activation of oxone for MB degradation.
Raja Deepika: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mathur Gopalakrishnan Sethuraman: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
